title: AURpocalypse now: a look at the recent AUR attacks
blurb: Attackers spun up new accounts to adopt orphaned Arch User Repository packages and push malware, forcing Arch to suspend new-user registration.

title: Nobel Winner John Jumper to Leave Google DeepMind for Anthropic
blurb: John Jumper, the Google DeepMind vice president who won the 2024 Nobel Prize in chemistry for AlphaFold, is leaving to join Anthropic. The move hands Anthropic one of the field's most prominent scientific researchers and marks another high-profile defection from DeepMind as frontier labs compete for top AI talent.

title: Satellite reveals immense scale of GPS signal tampering
blurb: Xona Space Systems' experimental Pulsar-0 satellite has mapped the scale of GPS jamming across Europe and the Middle East from orbit for the first time, finding interference more widespread than expected even at 310 miles up. The data shows low-Earth-orbit spacecraft also suffer degraded positioning, navigation, and timing signals, a problem Xona aims to address with a 300-satellite constellation broadcasting signals 100 times stronger than GPS.

title: ShinyHunters published 45GB of Madison Square Garden data, including facial recognition surveillance records
blurb: ShinyHunters published 45GB of data stolen from Madison Square Garden Entertainment after the company missed a June 15 ransom deadline, exposing facial recognition logs, biometric tracking records, and internal threat dossiers on attendees. The dump, drawn from what hackers claim are 26 million records, includes risk tags on celebrities and customer complaints about misidentification, and triggered a federal class action the next day.

title: Researcher turns wi-fi smart lightbulb into a Banned Book Library — open source project makes digital books available via a server and open Wi-Fi access point hacked into an ESP32-powered bulb
blurb: A security researcher reprogrammed a Tasmota-flashed ESP32C3 smart lightbulb into a stealth "banned book library," broadcasting an open Wi-Fi network and hosting a server that serves digital books from onboard storage. Inspired by Ben Brown's short story "Library," the open-source project fits nearly 2MB of compressed text into the bulb's 4MB flash and is available on Codeberg for anyone to replicate.

title: China unveils man-portable anti-drone laser that can burn through a drone 1,600 feet away in four seconds — backpack-sized 2-kilowatt weapon uses AI for targeting, weighs 55 pounds, and can be carried by a single soldier
blurb: Chinese defense supplier Harbin Xinguang demoed two backpack-sized anti-drone lasers, the Lijian II and Lijian III, at a Beijing arms expo, with the 25kg Lijian III burning through a drone at 500 meters in four seconds. The roughly 2-kilowatt units use AI for targeting and cost about $295,000 each, trading the wattage of mounted systems like Israel's 100kW Iron Beam for portability against small, low, slow drones.

title: Where to Find the Colors Your Screen Can't Show You
blurb: Most real-world cyans fall outside the gamut of standard RGB screens, which can only manipulate three cone types within a triangle that excludes the most saturated cyans, so a digital photo or game can never reproduce them. This post explains why human vision works through cone-cell response rather than wavelength, and shows how to find these unreproducible colors in the physical world without specialized equipment.

title: The Repair Nightmare that are Smart Rings
blurb: iFixit tore down the $400 Oura Ring 5 and found its 10.5 mAh battery effectively unreachable, with the Li-ion cell popping from the heat needed to pry the epoxy-sealed metal shell apart and the ring destroyed in the process. The teardown highlights a looming problem for smart rings under the EU's February 2027 user-replaceable-battery rule, which may force manufacturers to redesign the devices, seek an exemption, or exit the market.

title: The Market’s AI Fanfare Is Running Into a Harsh Political Reality
blurb: The Wall Street Journal argues that Anthropic's prospects for a blockbuster IPO may hinge as much on Washington politics as on investor demand. The piece frames mounting regulatory scrutiny and shifting policy winds as a risk to the AI market's rally, suggesting that election outcomes and federal AI rules could reshape valuations regardless of how strong the underlying business fundamentals look.

title: The developer behind VLC’s 6 billion downloads now wants to connect hundreds of millions of robots
blurb: VLC lead developer Jean-Baptiste Kempf raised $5M, led by Lightspeed, for Kyber, an open-source SDK that synchronizes video, audio, sensor data, and control inputs to operate remote machines at ultra-low latency. Built on FFmpeg and VLC, Kyber demonstrated 8ms glass-to-glass latency and is already deployed in defence, telco, and robotics, targeting fleets that today top out around 2,000 to 3,000 remote-driven vehicles.

title: Nobel Winner John Jumper to Leave Google DeepMind for Anthropic
blurb: John Jumper, the Google DeepMind VP who shared the 2024 Nobel Prize in chemistry for AlphaFold, is leaving the company to join Anthropic. The move brings one of the field's leading AI-for-science researchers to a lab better known for frontier language models, signaling a deeper push into scientific applications.

title: The Market’s AI Fanfare Is Running Into a Harsh Political Reality
blurb: The Wall Street Journal argues that the AI market's bullish momentum is colliding with political headwinds that could complicate the sector's biggest planned offerings. Anthropic's prospects for a blockbuster IPO may hinge as much on election outcomes and regulatory sentiment as on investor demand for AI exposure.

title: Norway imposes broad restrictions on AI for elementary school kids
blurb: Norway will bar elementary students from using generative AI tools starting in late August, citing concerns that AI lets children skip foundational steps in learning to read, write, and do math. The ban covers grades one through seven, requires teacher supervision for ages 14 to 16, and follows the country's 2024 classroom smartphone ban, which reduced bullying and improved grades.

title: Lightfield Just Assembled a Working CRM Live On Stage, Then Unstuck a Stalled Deal in 3 Minutes
blurb: Lightfield demoed an AI-native CRM that assembles itself from four connected sources: mail, calendar, a data warehouse, and a call recorder. No custom fields, no implementation project. Accounts arrived pre-enriched and opportunities were created automatically off emails and calls. In the demo, the system diagnosed a stalled enterprise deal by running code against every closed-won and closed-lost record, surfaced the pattern that won deals all had IT involved early, then found the CIO and drafted an intro email in about three minutes. Lightfield is now at roughly 3,000 customers.

title: The developer behind VLC’s 6 billion downloads now wants to connect hundreds of millions of robots
blurb: The lead developer of VLC, Jean-Baptiste Kempf, raised $5M led by Lightspeed for Kyber, an open-source SDK that synchronizes video, audio, sensor, and control data across remote machines at near-zero latency. Built on FFmpeg and VLC, the same stack behind 6 billion downloads, Kyber demoed 8 milliseconds of glass-to-glass latency and is already deployed in defence, telco, and robotics. The bet is that the hard problem only gets harder as fleets scale, since the largest remote-driving operations manage 2,000 to 3,000 vehicles today and reaching millions needs different infrastructure plus observability for both human operators and AI agents.

title: The Market’s AI Fanfare Is Running Into a Harsh Political Reality
blurb: Anthropic's path to a blockbuster IPO now runs through Washington as much as Wall Street, with the company's prospects tied to the political climate around AI regulation. The market's AI enthusiasm is colliding with a harder political reality, where election outcomes and policy fights could shape valuations more than investor appetite does. For founders riding the same wave, it is a reminder that the exit math for AI companies increasingly depends on variables no pitch deck controls.

title: Nobel Winner John Jumper to Leave Google DeepMind for Anthropic
blurb: John Jumper, the DeepMind VP who shared the 2024 Nobel Prize in chemistry for AlphaFold, is leaving Google DeepMind to join Anthropic. The defection moves one of the most decorated names in AI-for-science out of the lab that built it and into a company better known for frontier models and safety than for protein folding. Talent is the scarcest input in this race, and a Nobel laureate choosing Anthropic over Google signals where the most ambitious researchers now think the work, and the resources, are headed.

title: AURpocalypse now: a look at the recent AUR attacks
blurb: Attackers spun up new Arch User Repository accounts to adopt orphaned packages and push malicious updates that installed malware on users' systems. The AUR has no review process for new packages or updates and roughly 14,000 of its 107,000 packages are orphaned, so maintainers spent days manually pulling compromised packages and have now suspended new-user registration while they weigh a longer-term fix.

title: Where to Find the Colors Your Screen Can't Show You
blurb: An engineer explains why most real-world cyans fall outside the sRGB gamut your monitor can reproduce, walking through cone-cell response, the CIE chromaticity diagram, and how to actually find these colors in person. The piece is a clear primer on the limits of three-primary displays, useful grounding for designers who push saturated cyans and greens and wonder why they never look as intense on screen.

title: Satellite reveals immense scale of GPS signal tampering
blurb: Xona Space Systems' experimental Pulsar-0 satellite mapped GNSS jamming and spoofing across Europe and the Middle East from low Earth orbit for the first time, finding interference far more widespread than expected and degrading positioning even for spacecraft at 500 km altitude. Since GNSS signals underpin power grids, finance, and aviation, defenders relying on GPS for timing or navigation should assume PNT degradation in contested regions and build in non-GNSS fallback.

title: ShinyHunters published 45GB of Madison Square Garden data, including facial recognition surveillance records
blurb: ShinyHunters published 45GB of data stolen from Madison Square Garden Entertainment after the company missed a June 15 ransom deadline, exposing facial recognition surveillance logs, biometric tracking data, and internal threat dossiers on attendees from a claimed 26 million records. The dump includes risk profiles of celebrities and customer complaints about misidentification, and a federal class action was filed the day after the June 16 release.

title: AURpocalypse now: a look at the recent AUR attacks
blurb: Attackers created fresh AUR accounts to adopt orphaned packages and push malicious updates that installed malware via AUR helpers like paru and yay, exploiting the repository's lack of any review process for new or adopted packages. Arch maintainers spent days removing compromised packages and have suspended new-user registration, but with 14,000 orphaned packages up for grabs and no vetting model, AUR users should audit recently installed or updated PKGBUILDs.

title: Hackers Exploit Gravity SMTP WordPress Plugin Bug to Expose API Keys
blurb: Threat actors are actively exploiting CVE-2026-4020 (CVSS 5.3), an unauthenticated information-disclosure flaw in the Gravity SMTP WordPress plugin, where a REST API endpoint with a permission_callback that always returns true leaks a full System Report including live Amazon SES, Google, Mailjet, and Zoho API keys. Wordfence has blocked over 17M exploit attempts since early May; sites should update to version 2.1.5 and rotate all exposed email-integration credentials.

title: ShinyHunters published 45GB of Madison Square Garden data, including facial recognition surveillance records
blurb: ShinyHunters published 45GB of data stolen from Madison Square Garden Entertainment after the company missed a June 15 ransom deadline, exposing facial recognition surveillance logs, internal celebrity threat dossiers, and personal records tied to as many as 26 million visitors. A federal class action followed a day later, putting MSG's years-long biometric screening program, already used to bar lawyers from firms suing the company, at the center of a major privacy liability case.
